title: VOIP-Anchored Synthetic Identity with Gradual Credit Building Phase
narrative: A synthetic identity is established using a fabricated SSN linked to a newly provisioned VOIP number from Bandwidth.com or similar provider. The fraudster opens a secured credit card with $500 limit, makes 8-12 small legitimate purchases ($15-$45 each) over 90 days to establish payment history. Phone carrier lookup returns VOIP classification with activation date within 6 months. After the nurture period, the identity applies for unsecured credit, receives $3,500 limit, and immediately executes bust-out: 4 transactions totaling $3,200 within 72 hours at high-risk MCCs (5732 electronics, 5944 jewelry). Email domain is a free provider (gmail.com, yahoo.com) created within same 30-day window as phone provisioning. No device fingerprint history exists prior to first application. Address resolves to mail drop service (CMRA) rather than residential dwelling.
indicators: Phone number carrier lookup returns VOIP provider with service activation <180 days prior to application
card1_txn_count_7day = 0 followed by card1_txn_count_30day jumping to 8-12 transactions in $15-$45 range
P_emaildomain creation date within 45 days of phone provisioning date per WHOIS/account metadata
addr1 resolves to commercial mail receiving agency (CMRA) rather than residential address type
Bust-out signature: TransactionAmt sum >85% of credit limit across 3-5 transactions within 72 hours after 90-day dormancy
Device fingerprint first-seen timestamp matches application date, no prior browsing/session history
All transactions occur at MCC 5732, 5944, or 5661 (high-resale categories) with is_night=1 for 60%+ of volume
summary: A new identity uses a VOIP phone number and mail drop address to open a secured credit card, makes 8-12 small purchases over 90 days to build history, then maxes out a new unsecured card with large electronics and jewelry purchases within 72 hours.
reasoning: The fraudster is manufacturing a credible credit profile using fake credentials to qualify for higher credit limits, then immediately cashing out before the synthetic identity collapses—normal customers don't combine brand-new VOIP numbers, mail drops, and sudden spending spikes after careful payment behavior.
indicator_explanations: Phone service is brand new and comes from a disposable VOIP provider
Card goes from zero activity to sudden regular purchases after initial silence
Email account was created at the same time as the phone number
Address is a mailbox service, not where someone actually lives
After months of inactivity, card is maxed out in a frenzied spending spree
Device appears for the first time at application with no prior usage history
Purchases concentrate on easily resold goods and happen mostly at night
